title: Agitation, tremor, and a prolonged QT two days after a fall

chart.oneLiner: 62M with HTN, CKD, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, and depression — admitted 2 days ago after a fall — becomes acutely agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic, and confused with visual hallucinations; he is on a long list of QT-prolonging and anticholinergic home medications.

opening: Get them off me — there are bugs crawling all over the walls! Why have you got me tied down here?
patientFile:
PERSONA: 62-year-old man, agitated, tremulous, profusely diaphoretic (soaking the sheets), disoriented, picking at the bedclothes, frightened by visual hallucinations of insects. Poor historian who answers in disjointed, hallucination-tinged fragments.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks family/nursing/chart/medication reconciliation/what happened): Admitted 2 days ago after a mechanical fall at home. His wife reports he drinks heavily and daily — roughly a pint of vodka every day for years — and his LAST DRINK was the evening before admission, so about 2 days (~48 h) ago. Yesterday he had a fine tremor and was anxious and sweaty; overnight this escalated into agitation, drenching sweats, and seeing bugs. He had ONE withdrawal seizure during a previous hospital admission a few years ago. His home medications were continued on admission and his evening doses were given. No new fever source has been found so far. The fall was unwitnessed; he was found on the floor and there is a bruise over the right temple.

HPI (from patient — limited): agitated, not oriented to place or time, keeps reporting bugs/insects on the walls and on his skin, swatting at them.

PMH (reveal on direct questioning / chart review): hypertension; chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine ~1.6); atrial fibrillation; depression; prior alcohol-withdrawal seizure. SOCIAL: heavy daily alcohol use (a pint of vodka/day), ~40 pack-year smoker, lives with his wife. FAMILY: noncontributory. ROS (collateral): drenching sweats, tremor, poor oral intake and almost no food for several days before admission.

MEDS (home list, all continued in hospital — reveal on medication reconciliation): warfarin (for atrial fibrillation), metoprolol, hydrochlorothiazide, amitriptyline (for depression/sleep), quetiapine at bedtime (for sleep/agitation), gabapentin, and acetaminophen as needed for pain. NO regular benzodiazepine. NO thiamine has been given.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: agitated, drenched in sweat, coarse resting tremor, hypervigilant, swatting at unseen objects.
- Vitals: BP 172/98, HR 118, RR 24, 38.2°C, SpO2 96% RA (autonomic hyperactivity; tachycardia present DESPITE home beta-blocker).
- Skin: warm and SOAKED with sweat (NOT dry/flushed); right temporal bruise from the fall; no track marks; no rash.
- HEENT: pupils mid-size and reactive (NOT widely dilated); mucous membranes dry; no tongue laceration.
- Neuro: disoriented, coarse tremor, visual hallucinations; no focal motor deficit; reflexes symmetric; no neck stiffness; gait not tested (too agitated/unsafe).
- No nystagmus or gaze palsy elicited on brief exam; no obvious ataxia tested.
- Cardiac: irregularly irregular, tachycardic; no murmur. Lungs: clear.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- The patient is agitated/confused: give disjointed, hallucination-tinged answers, mention the bugs. Provide COLLATERAL (heavy daily drinking, last drink ~2 days ago, prior withdrawal seizure, the fall, the full home medication list, no thiamine given) ONLY when the student explicitly asks family/nursing/the chart/medication reconciliation/what happened.
- The skin is DRENCHED in sweat and the pupils are NOT widely dilated — reveal these (which argue AGAINST a dry anticholinergic toxidrome) only when the student examines the skin/pupils or asks.
- Reveal exam findings only when the relevant exam is performed or asked about; otherwise give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.Glucose: Fingerstick glucose 88 — normal (excludes hypoglycemia as the cause of agitation).
labs.BMP: Na 128 (low), K 3.0 (low), Cl 92, HCO3 16 (low), BUN 38, Cr 2.1 (up from baseline 1.6 — AKI on CKD), glucose 88. Anion gap = Na − (Cl + HCO3) = 128 − (92 + 16) = 20 (elevated, normal ≤12) → high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis. The hyponatremia fits the thiazide; the AKI is prerenal from poor intake and diaphoresis.
labs.Mg_Phos: Mg 1.2 (low), Phos 1.6 (low) — classic alcohol-related depletion; both also prolong the QT.
labs.VBG_lactate: Venous pH 7.30, lactate 4.2 (elevated) with serum beta-hydroxybutyrate positive — alcoholic ketoacidosis plus lactic acidosis account for the elevated anion gap of 20; osmolar gap not elevated (no toxic alcohol).
labs.CBC: WBC 9.8 (no leukocytosis), Hgb 12.4, Plt 96 (low) — thrombocytopenia from alcohol/marrow effect, relevant given the head injury.
labs.LFTs: AST 142, ALT 61 (AST:ALT > 2:1 pattern), GGT elevated, albumin 3.2 — alcoholic liver pattern.
labs.Coags: INR 4.6 (supratherapeutic on warfarin), so the CT head was urgent given the fall and the bruise — there is a real bleeding risk.
labs.Infection_workup: Blood/urine cultures, UA, CXR sent to exclude an infectious precipitant — UA bland, no clear source; cultures pending.
labs.CT_head: Non-contrast CT head (urgent given supratherapeutic INR + fall + bruise + confusion): NO acute intracranial hemorrhage and no acute infarct; age-appropriate atrophy only. This excludes a traumatic subdural/intracranial bleed as the driver.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Tachycardia (~115) with a markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~520 ms); broad-based T waves. The prolongation is driven by his anticholinergic/QT-prolonging home medications (amitriptyline, quetiapine) compounded by hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia — a real torsades risk.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus/atrial tachycardia with markedly prolonged QTc (~520 ms) from drug effect plus hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing tachycardia with a markedly long QT interval (drug-induced / electrolyte-related)
searchTerms: long QT interval ECG drug induced
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 62-year-old man with heavy alcohol use, on amitriptyline and quetiapine, with low potassium and magnesium, presents agitated and tremulous; his rhythm strip shows a strikingly long QT.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Clear lung fields; no focal consolidation; obtained to exclude aspiration/pneumonia as an infectious precipitant of the delirium.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal PA chest film.
cxr.imageDescription: Clear chest radiograph (no definite consolidation), screening for an infectious precipitant.
searchTerms: normal PA chest radiograph clear lungs
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 62-year-old man with alcohol use presents agitated and febrile, and a film is obtained to exclude aspiration or pneumonia.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your assessment of this agitated, hallucinating, post-fall inpatient, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis). Name at least THREE distinct mechanisms/etiologies of an agitated, hyperadrenergic delirium that you must distinguish here.
steps.rubric: Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens (leading,6). Anticholinergic / drug toxidrome from his home meds — amitriptyline + quetiapine (CANT-MISS,4). Intracranial process — traumatic subdural/ICH after the fall, especially on warfarin with a supratherapeutic INR (CANT-MISS,4). Infection / sepsis precipitating delirium (CANT-MISS,3). Hypoglycemia (CANT-MISS,2). Wernicke encephalopathy / Other: serotonin or sedative withdrawal, electrolyte/metabolic encephalopathy (core,2). Names THREE distinct mechanisms of hyperadrenergic agitated delirium — e.g. neurotransmitter rebound in withdrawal, anticholinergic excess, structural/traumatic brain injury, systemic infection, metabolic derangement (core,2). Named leading dx w/ justification — heavy daily drinking, last drink ~48 h ago, drenching diaphoresis, coarse tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, visual hallucinations, prior withdrawal seizure (core,2). PENALTY: anchoring on withdrawal without considering the drug toxidrome or the post-fall bleed on warfarin (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens. The genuinely competing diagnosis is an anticholinergic/drug toxidrome from his continued home amitriptyline and quetiapine — it also causes agitation, hyperthermia, tachycardia, and hallucinations, and it is what is prolonging his QT. Must-not-miss alternatives: a traumatic intracranial bleed (subdural/ICH) after the unwitnessed fall — especially dangerous because he is on warfarin with a supratherapeutic INR; infection/sepsis precipitating delirium; and hypoglycemia. Also consider Wernicke encephalopathy, sedative/serotonergic effects, and electrolyte/metabolic encephalopathy. Three distinct mechanisms to distinguish: (1) neurotransmitter rebound/autonomic hyperactivity of alcohol withdrawal, (2) anticholinergic excess from medications, and (3) structural/traumatic brain injury — with systemic infection and metabolic derangement as further mechanisms. The features favoring withdrawal over the anticholinergic toxidrome are the DRENCHING diaphoresis (anticholinergic delirium is dry) and the mid-size reactive pupils (not widely dilated), plus the clear timeline from the last drink and the prior withdrawal seizure.

criticalActions.item: Anticholinergic / drug toxidrome from amitriptyline + quetiapine
Intracranial process — traumatic subdural/ICH after the fall on warfarin

coreActions.item: Wernicke encephalopathy / sedative-serotonergic or metabolic encephalopathy
Names three distinct mechanisms of hyperadrenergic agitated delirium
Named leading dx w/ justification — last drink ~48 h ago, diaphoresis, tremor, autonomic hyperactivity, hallucinations, prior withdrawal seizure

penalties.item: Anchoring on withdrawal without considering the drug toxidrome or the post-fall bleed on warfarin

mustNotMiss: Anticholinergic / drug toxidrome from amitriptyline + quetiapine
Intracranial process — traumatic subdural/ICH after the fall on warfarin

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Fingerstick glucose (CANT-MISS,3). BMP with magnesium, potassium, phosphate — and note the anion gap (CANT-MISS,3). Non-contrast CT head given the fall + confusion + warfarin (CANT-MISS,3). Coags/INR given warfarin and the head injury (2). VBG + lactate + ketones/beta-hydroxybutyrate to work up the anion-gap acidosis (1). EKG to assess the QT given his QT-prolonging meds and low K/Mg (1). CBC and LFTs (1). Infection screen — cultures/UA/CXR (1). Medication reconciliation + CIWA-Ar scoring (bonus). PENALTY: anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection, intracranial bleed, or the drug effect (-2); giving glucose before thiamine in the orders (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: fingerstick glucose; BMP (note the anion gap) with magnesium, potassium, phosphate; coags/INR (warfarin + head injury); non-contrast CT head (fall + confusion + supratherapeutic INR); VBG with lactate and serum beta-hydroxybutyrate to characterize the anion-gap acidosis; EKG (assess the QT given amitriptyline/quetiapine and low K/Mg); CBC; LFTs; infection workup (cultures/UA/CXR); CXR. Reconcile the home medication list and start CIWA-Ar scoring.

criticalActions.item: BMP with magnesium, potassium, phosphate and the anion gap
Non-contrast CT head given the fall + confusion + warfarin

coreActions.item: Coags / INR given warfarin and the head injury
VBG + lactate + ketones to work up the anion-gap acidosis
EKG to assess the QT given QT-prolonging meds and low K/Mg

penalties.item: Anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection, intracranial bleed, or the drug effect

requiredTests: BMP with magnesium, potassium, phosphate and the anion gap
Non-contrast CT head given the fall + confusion + warfarin
optionalTests: Coags / INR given warfarin and the head injury
VBG + lactate + ketones to work up the anion-gap acidosis
EKG to assess the QT given QT-prolonging meds and low K/Mg
contraindicatedTests: Anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection, intracranial bleed, or the drug effect
orderSensitiveActions: Anchoring on withdrawal without excluding infection, intracranial bleed, or the drug effect

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's OWN 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and state why they matter for how you manage him.
steps.rubric: Findings = tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~520 ms). CREDIT: identifies the prolonged QT/QTc (7); measures or estimates the QTc and recognizes the torsades de pointes risk (4); attributes it to the QT-prolonging anticholinergic meds (amitriptyline/quetiapine) compounded by hypokalemia + hypomagnesemia (4). PENALTY: calling the QT normal / missing it (-3).
criticalFindings: Markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~520 ms) with tachycardia — drug- and electrolyte-related, carrying a torsades de pointes risk.
commonMistakes: calling the QT normal or reading only the rate
not connecting the long QT to the amitriptyline/quetiapine and the low K/Mg
steps.idealAnswer: Tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT — QTc roughly 520 ms with broad-based T waves. This is a high-risk tracing: the prolongation is driven by his QT-prolonging anticholinergic home medications (amitriptyline and quetiapine) compounded by hypokalemia (K 3.0) and hypomagnesemia (Mg 1.2), and it puts him at risk for torsades de pointes. This directly changes management: aggressively replete magnesium and potassium, hold/discontinue the offending amitriptyline and quetiapine, and avoid additional QT-prolonging agents — and it is another reason to favor benzodiazepines over antipsychotics for his agitation.

criticalActions: Markedly prolonged QT (QTc ~520 ms) with torsades risk — drug- and electrolyte-related.
coreActions: attributes the long QT to amitriptyline/quetiapine plus hypokalemia and hypomagnesemia

penalties.label: calls the QT normal / misses the prolonged QT

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = clear film, no focal consolidation. CREDIT: identifies a clear/near-clear film (6); notes it screens for an infectious precipitant/aspiration of the delirium (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: clear lung fields, no focal consolidation
criticalFindings: Clear chest film with no focal consolidation; obtained to exclude an infectious precipitant/aspiration.
commonMistakes: inventing pathology on a clear film
steps.idealAnswer: Clear lung fields with no focal consolidation or effusion; obtained to exclude pneumonia or aspiration as an infectious precipitant of the delirium.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: clear chest film, no focal consolidation; screens for an infectious precipitant/aspiration.

coreActions.item: Notes it screens for an infectious precipitant/aspiration

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: With a normal glucose, a CT head showing no bleed, a markedly prolonged QT on the EKG, and these labs, commit to your diagnosis. Explain how you resolved the alcohol-withdrawal vs anticholinergic-toxidrome fork and what residual issues you must still manage.
steps.rubric: Delirium tremens / severe alcohol withdrawal as the leading diagnosis (6). Resolves the fork toward withdrawal: drenching diaphoresis and mid-size reactive pupils argue AGAINST a dry anticholinergic toxidrome, plus the clear timeline from the last drink (~48 h) and prior withdrawal seizure; names the discriminator (3). Notes residual concurrent problems still requiring action — the drug-induced long QT with torsades risk, hyponatremia/hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia/hypophosphatemia, AKI on CKD, the anion-gap acidosis (AKA + lactate), supratherapeutic INR, and that DTs is a medical emergency with significant mortality (bonus,1).
steps.idealAnswer: Diagnosis: Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens. A heavy daily drinker whose last drink was ~48 h ago now has autonomic hyperactivity (tachycardia despite a beta-blocker, hypertension, fever, drenching diaphoresis), a coarse tremor, and visual hallucinations, with a prior withdrawal seizure. I resolve the withdrawal-vs-anticholinergic-toxidrome fork toward withdrawal because his skin is DRENCHED in sweat and his pupils are mid-size and reactive — an anticholinergic toxidrome is classically DRY (anhidrotic) with widely dilated pupils — and the timeline from the last drink and the prior withdrawal seizure fit withdrawal. The normal glucose excludes hypoglycemia and the CT head excludes a post-fall intracranial bleed. But this is not a clean single problem: his medications and electrolytes have given him a markedly prolonged QT (torsades risk) that I must treat, and he concurrently has hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypophosphatemia, AKI on CKD, a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis from alcoholic ketoacidosis plus lactate (gap 20), and a supratherapeutic INR on warfarin after a head injury. DTs is a medical emergency with significant mortality and must be treated aggressively.

bonusActions.item: Notes DTs is a medical emergency with significant mortality and lists the concurrent problems (long QT, electrolytes, AKI, acidosis, INR)

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan? Include how the prolonged QT changes your choices, and address safe disposition and follow-up for this patient.
steps.rubric: Per MGH alcohol withdrawal: symptom-triggered benzodiazepines per CIWA-Ar — lorazepam/diazepam, escalate as needed (CANT-MISS,6); thiamine BEFORE any glucose to prevent Wernicke (CANT-MISS,4); aggressively replete magnesium and potassium AND hold the QT-prolonging amitriptyline/quetiapine to reduce torsades risk — prefer benzodiazepines over antipsychotics for agitation (CANT-MISS,4); repair phosphate, treat the hyponatremia/AKI and give folate/multivitamin (2); reverse/correct the supratherapeutic INR (vitamin K +/- 4-factor PCC given the head injury) and re-image if any deterioration (2); escalate to ICU/phenobarbital for severe DTs and monitor on telemetry for seizures and torsades (2); safe disposition — admit (ICU for severe DTs/QT), telemetry, medication reconciliation removing/avoiding offending agents, fall precautions, repeat electrolytes/QTc, and arrange AUD treatment + close follow-up before any discharge (3). PENALTY: glucose before thiamine (-3); giving an antipsychotic (haloperidol/quetiapine) that further prolongs the QT or under-treating severe DTs (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Symptom-triggered benzodiazepines guided by CIWA-Ar (lorazepam or diazepam), escalating doses for severe DTs; consider ICU and phenobarbital for refractory cases. Give thiamine BEFORE any glucose (prevent Wernicke), plus folate and a multivitamin. Because the EKG shows a markedly prolonged QT with torsades risk, aggressively replete magnesium and potassium, repair phosphate, HOLD the QT-prolonging anticholinergic agents (amitriptyline and quetiapine), avoid adding QT-prolonging antipsychotics such as haloperidol, and prefer benzodiazepines for agitation; keep him on telemetry. Treat the hyponatremia and prerenal AKI with judicious fluids and correct the anion-gap acidosis (it resolves with dextrose-after-thiamine, fluids, and treating ketoacidosis). Correct the supratherapeutic INR with vitamin K (and 4-factor PCC if any bleeding/deterioration) given the recent head injury, with a low threshold to re-image. Monitor for withdrawal seizures and torsades. Disposition: ADMIT, ICU if severe DTs or unstable QT; telemetry; medication reconciliation that removes the offending QT-prolonging/anticholinergic drugs; fall precautions; serial CIWA-Ar, electrolytes, and QTc. Before any eventual discharge, arrange safe disposition — alcohol-use-disorder treatment/counseling, thiamine and electrolyte follow-up, a corrected/managed medication list, caregiver education for his wife, and close outpatient follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Replete Mg/K and hold QT-prolonging amitriptyline/quetiapine — prefer benzodiazepines over antipsychotics

coreActions.item: Repair phosphate, treat hyponatremia/AKI, give folate/multivitamin
Correct supratherapeutic INR (vitamin K +/- PCC) given the head injury; re-image if deterioration
Escalate to ICU/phenobarbital for severe DTs; telemetry; monitor for seizures and torsades
Safe disposition — admit/ICU, telemetry, med reconciliation, fall precautions, AUD treatment and close follow-up before discharge

bonusActions.item: Address the underlying alcohol use disorder with caregiver education

penalties.item: Giving a QT-prolonging antipsychotic (haloperidol/quetiapine) or under-treating severe DTs

physicalExamMappings.finding: General: agitated, drenched in sweat, coarse resting tremor, hypervigilant, swatting at unseen objects.
Skin: warm and SOAKED with sweat (not dry/flushed); right temporal bruise from the fall — drenching diaphoresis argues against a dry anticholinergic toxidrome.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Skin warm and dry; no bruising.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Neuro: disoriented, coarse tremor, visual hallucinations; no focal motor deficit; no neck stiffness.
Neuro: no focal motor deficit; reflexes symmetric; no pronator drift — argues against a lateralizing intracranial lesion.
No nuchal rigidity — argues against meningitis as the cause of the delirium.

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 62M with HTN, CKD, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, and depression — admitted 2 days ago after an unwitnessed fall — becomes acutely agitated, tremulous, diaphoretic, and confused with visual hallucinations ~48 h after his last drink, while on a long list of QT-prolonging anticholinergic home medications. The diagnosis is Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens, reached by eliciting collateral and reconciling medications, performing the targeted exam (drenching diaphoresis and reactive pupils that distinguish it from a dry anticholinergic toxidrome), and interpreting a workup that excludes hypoglycemia, a post-fall intracranial bleed, and infection while revealing concurrent multi-system problems — a drug/electrolyte-induced long QT, hyponatremia, hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia/hypophosphatemia, AKI on CKD, a high-anion-gap acidosis (AKA + lactate), and a supratherapeutic INR.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: General: agitated, drenched in sweat, coarse resting tremor, hypervigilant, swatting at unseen objects.
Skin: warm and SOAKED with sweat (not dry/flushed); right temporal bruise from the fall — drenching diaphoresis argues against a dry anticholinergic toxidrome.
Neuro: disoriented, coarse tremor, visual hallucinations; no focal motor deficit; no neck stiffness.
Neuro: no focal motor deficit; reflexes symmetric; no pronator drift — argues against a lateralizing intracranial lesion.
No nuchal rigidity — argues against meningitis as the cause of the delirium.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting tachycardia despite a home beta-blocker.
Elicit collateral and reconcile the home medications, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam — the drenching diaphoresis and reactive pupils distinguish withdrawal from a dry anticholinergic toxidrome.

Build a differential led by Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens while explicitly weighing the competing anticholinergic/drug toxidrome and excluding a post-fall intracranial bleed (on warfarin), infection, and hypoglycemia.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup; interpret the patient's OWN EKG (markedly prolonged QT) and CXR and the multi-system labs.
Commit to Alcohol withdrawal / delirium tremens, resolve the fork, and manage the DTs plus the concurrent long QT, electrolyte, AKI, acidosis, and INR problems, with safe disposition and follow-up.

Anchoring on withdrawal without excluding the post-fall bleed on warfarin or the drug toxidrome

imageInterpretationPearls: When a patient is on QT-prolonging drugs with low K/Mg, measure the QTc — a long QT is an interpretation task that directly changes management (replete electrolytes, hold offending drugs, avoid antipsychotics).
An incidental abnormality on the patient's own study is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically and state pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Uses an open-ended opening question and orients the agitated patient calmly
Shows empathy for the patient's fear and seeks collateral from family/nursing
Asks permission before sensitive questions (alcohol use)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
